title: 有翼之暗
summary:
Mochizuki and the narrator discuss inconsistencies regarding the death of Yasuko (Ito). Mochizuki argues that if the crime scene were part of the killer's original plan, Yasuko should have been killed at "Hell's Gate" rather than in her own room. He suggests the killer may have been interrupted by Uma, though he admits his deduction might be incomplete. Mochizuki expresses a somber, philosophical view that deductive reasoning is insufficient for understanding the case, comparing the attempt to apply logic to the crime to using Newtonian mechanics to control nuclear physics.

A housekeeper notifies the group that dinner is ready, and Arai invites the investigators to dine with the family. The dining room in the Inoue household is an extravagantly decorated, massive space featuring a vaulted ceiling with religious art, a Raphael painting of the Virgin Mary, and a large chandelier, reflecting a mixture of various Western and Southern European styles.

The dinner guests include Akiyama, Kouichi, Akiyoshi, Fujiko, Shin'ichi, Yuzuha, Muto, the narrator, Satsuki, Yuki, and Arai. The two daughters of the victim, Hidemasa, are notably absent. During the meal, Arai introduces the attendees. When Akiyama asks Wakaba about the progress of the investigation, Wakaba responds cryptically. Tensions arise when Yuki sharply demands that the killer be found quickly to prove his innocence, prompting a reproach from Wakaba. Despite these underlying frictions, the meal proceeds in a heavy, largely silent atmosphere of indifference.
